subject: Chemistry
title: Van der Waals Forces: The Weakest Bonds
description: A fast breakdown of Van der Waals Forces: The Weakest Bonds for builders.

hook.text: Geckos climb glass walls using Van der Waals forces—the weakest attraction between atoms, but billions at once.

buildup.text: Even nonpolar molecules have fleeting charge imbalances as electrons move. These create temporary tiny dipoles.

discovery.text: A temporary dipole in one molecule induces an opposite dipole in its neighbor. They briefly attract—then shift again.

twist.text: These forces are incredibly weak alone. But gecko toe pads have billions of tiny hairs, each adding Van der Waals pull.

climax.text: Van der Waals forces explain why noble gases can be liquefied at extreme cold—even with no chemical bonds.

punchline.text: Fleeting, tiny, everywhere. The weakest force holds geckos to ceilings.

quiz.question: How do geckos climb smooth glass surfaces?
options: Billions of tiny hairs generate enough Van der Waals force to support their weight
They secrete a sticky adhesive from their feet
Their feet create a vacuum seal against the surface
